cues.label: High-level map
cues.headline: Text becomes a table of numbers
cues.narration: An LLM does not read words. Tokenization splits text into pieces, each piece gets an ID, and each ID selects a learned vector — a table of numbers the transformer can process.

cues.label: Analogy
cues.headline: The tokenizer is a workshop station
cues.narration: Picture the pipeline as a workshop line. The tokenizer station receives text, checks its label, changes it into IDs, and hands forward a receipt saying exactly what changed.

cues.label: Tiny example
cues.headline: Follow one concrete input
cues.narration: Take one small input and follow it. In 'she saw the saw', the word 'saw' appears twice, becomes the same token ID both times, and each ID looks up the same embedding row.

cues.label: Mechanism
cues.headline: IDs index rows; length costs compute
cues.narration: Each token ID is a row index into the embedding matrix, and the row's numbers carry learned meaning. Sequence length is a real compute cost — more tokens means more work.

cues.label: Implementation
cues.headline: Encode and decode are exact inverses
cues.narration: In code, expect small inputs, named variables, and simple assertions. Build a vocab, encode text to IDs, decode IDs back to text, and assert that decode(encode(x)) == x.

cues.label: Misconception
cues.headline: Don't confuse the label with the object
cues.narration: Separate nearby ideas: a token is not a word, an ID is not its vector, a score is not a probability, and a high-level phase is not the detailed operation inside it.

cues.label: Synthesis
cues.headline: Return to the map, then practice
cues.narration: Return to the route: the audio gives the map, the visual makes it visible, the text defines it, and the code proves it. You can now explain text-to-tokens from every angle.
